question: B-4. Let \( n \) be an integer greater than 1. Show that there exists a polynomial \( P(x, y, z) \) with integral coefficients such that \( x \equiv P\left(x^{n}, x^{n+1}, x+x^{n+2}\right) \).
solution:
B-4 Let \( x=t^{n}, y=t^{n+1}, z=t+t^{n+2} \). We construct a polynomial \( P(x, y, z) \) with integral coefficients such that \( P(x, y, z)=t \). We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
z=t+t^{n+2} \\
z y=t^{n+2}+t^{2 n+3} \\
z y^{2}=t^{2 n+3}+t^{3 n+4} \\
\cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \\
z^{n-2}=t^{n^{2}-n-1}+t^{n^{2}}
\end{array}
\]

Multiply the above equations alternately by +1 and -1 and add:
\[
z\left[1-y+y^{2}-\cdots+(-1)^{n-2} y^{n-2}\right]=t+(-1)^{n-2} t^{n^{2}}=t+(-1)^{n} x^{n}
\]

Hence, if we define
\[
P(x, y, x)=z\left[\sum_{1=0}^{n-2}(-1)^{i} y^{i}\right]+(-1)^{n-1} x^{n}
\]

Then \( P\left(t^{n}, t^{n+1}, t+t^{n+2}\right)=t \).

descriptive_long.question: B-4. Let \( exponent \) be an integer greater than 1. Show that there exists a polynomial \( polyfunc(firstvar, secondvar, thirdvar) \) with integral coefficients such that \( firstvar \equiv polyfunc\left(firstvar^{exponent}, firstvar^{exponent+1}, firstvar+firstvar^{exponent+2}\right) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-4 Let \( firstvar=parameter^{exponent}, secondvar=parameter^{exponent+1}, thirdvar=parameter+parameter^{exponent+2} \). We construct a polynomial \( polyfunc(firstvar, secondvar, thirdvar) \) with integral coefficients such that \( polyfunc(firstvar, secondvar, thirdvar)=parameter \). We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
thirdvar=parameter+parameter^{exponent+2} \\
thirdvar\, secondvar=parameter^{exponent+2}+parameter^{2\, exponent+3} \\
thirdvar\, secondvar^{2}=parameter^{2\, exponent+3}+parameter^{3\, exponent+4} \\
\cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \\
thirdvar^{exponent-2}=parameter^{exponent^{2}-exponent-1}+parameter^{exponent^{2}}
\end{array}
\]

Multiply the above equations alternately by +1 and -1 and add:
\[
thirdvar\left[1-secondvar+secondvar^{2}-\cdots+(-1)^{exponent-2} secondvar^{exponent-2}\right]=parameter+(-1)^{exponent-2} parameter^{exponent^{2}}=parameter+(-1)^{exponent} firstvar^{exponent}
\]

Hence, if we define
\[
polyfunc(firstvar, secondvar, firstvar)=thirdvar\left[\sum_{indexer=0}^{exponent-2}(-1)^{indexer} secondvar^{indexer}\right]+(-1)^{exponent-1} firstvar^{exponent}
\]

Then \( polyfunc\left(parameter^{exponent}, parameter^{exponent+1}, parameter+parameter^{exponent+2}\right)=parameter \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-4. Let \( skyscraper \) be an integer greater than 1. Show that there exists a polynomial \( waterfall(meadowlark, sailcloth, breadcrumb) \) with integral coefficients such that \( meadowlark \equiv waterfall\left(meadowlark^{skyscraper}, meadowlark^{skyscraper+1}, meadowlark+meadowlark^{skyscraper+2}\right) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4 Let \( meadowlark=lanternfly^{skyscraper},\; sailcloth=lanternfly^{skyscraper+1},\; breadcrumb=lanternfly+lanternfly^{skyscraper+2} \). We construct a polynomial \( waterfall(meadowlark, sailcloth, breadcrumb) \) with integral coefficients such that \( waterfall(meadowlark, sailcloth, breadcrumb)=lanternfly \). We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
breadcrumb=lanternfly+lanternfly^{skyscraper+2} \\
breadcrumb\,sailcloth=lanternfly^{skyscraper+2}+lanternfly^{2\skyscraper+3} \\
breadcrumb\,sailcloth^{2}=lanternfly^{2\skyscraper+3}+lanternfly^{3\skyscraper+4} \\
\cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \\
breadcrumb^{\skyscraper-2}=lanternfly^{\skyscraper^{2}-\skyscraper-1}+lanternfly^{\skyscraper^{2}}
\end{array}
\]

Multiply the above equations alternately by \(+1\) and \(-1\) and add:
\[
breadcrumb\left[1-sailcloth+sailcloth^{2}-\cdots+(-1)^{\skyscraper-2}sailcloth^{\skyscraper-2}\right]
=lanternfly+(-1)^{\skyscraper-2}lanternfly^{\skyscraper^{2}}
=lanternfly+(-1)^{\skyscraper}meadowlark^{\skyscraper}
\]

Hence, if we define
\[
waterfall(meadowlark, sailcloth, meadowlark)=
breadcrumb\left[\sum_{1=0}^{\skyscraper-2}(-1)^{firestone}sailcloth^{firestone}\right]
+(-1)^{\skyscraper-1}meadowlark^{\skyscraper}
\]

then \( waterfall\left(lanternfly^{\skyscraper},\; lanternfly^{\skyscraper+1},\; lanternfly+lanternfly^{\skyscraper+2}\right)=lanternfly \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-4. Let \( fractionalvalue \) be an integer greater than 1. Show that there exists a polynomial \( irrationalfunc(knownvalue, horizontalaxis, groundlevel) \) with integral coefficients such that \( knownvalue \equiv irrationalfunc\left(knownvalue^{fractionalvalue}, knownvalue^{fractionalvalue+1}, knownvalue+knownvalue^{fractionalvalue+2}\right) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4 Let \( knownvalue=spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue}, horizontalaxis=spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+1}, groundlevel=spacecoordinate+spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+2} \). We construct a polynomial \( irrationalfunc(knownvalue, horizontalaxis, groundlevel) \) with integral coefficients such that \( irrationalfunc(knownvalue, horizontalaxis, groundlevel)=spacecoordinate \). We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
groundlevel=spacecoordinate+spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+2} \\
groundlevel\,horizontalaxis=spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+2}+spacecoordinate^{2 fractionalvalue+3} \\
groundlevel\,horizontalaxis^{2}=spacecoordinate^{2 fractionalvalue+3}+spacecoordinate^{3 fractionalvalue+4} \\
\cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \\
groundlevel^{fractionalvalue-2}=spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue^{2}-fractionalvalue-1}+spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue^{2}}
\end{array}
\]

Multiply the above equations alternately by +1 and -1 and add:
\[
groundlevel\left[1-horizontalaxis+horizontalaxis^{2}-\cdots+(-1)^{fractionalvalue-2} horizontalaxis^{fractionalvalue-2}\right]=spacecoordinate+(-1)^{fractionalvalue-2} spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue^{2}}=spacecoordinate+(-1)^{fractionalvalue} knownvalue^{fractionalvalue}
\]

Hence, if we define
\[
irrationalfunc(knownvalue, horizontalaxis, knownvalue)=groundlevel\left[\sum_{1=0}^{fractionalvalue-2}(-1)^{counterstop} horizontalaxis^{counterstop}\right]+(-1)^{fractionalvalue-1} knownvalue^{fractionalvalue}
\]

Then \( irrationalfunc\left(spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue}, spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+1}, spacecoordinate+spacecoordinate^{fractionalvalue+2}\right)=spacecoordinate \).

garbled_string.question: B-4. Let \( asdfghjkl \) be an integer greater than 1. Show that there exists a polynomial \( mnbvcxzlk(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, fvdqmscie) \) with integral coefficients such that \( qzxwvtnp \equiv mnbvcxzlk\left(qzxwvtnp^{asdfghjkl}, qzxwvtnp^{asdfghjkl+1}, qzxwvtnp+qzxwvtnp^{asdfghjkl+2}\right) \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-4 Let \( qzxwvtnp = lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl}, hjgrksla = lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+1}, fvdqmscie = lkjhpoyui + lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+2} \). We construct a polynomial \( mnbvcxzlk(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, fvdqmscie) \) with integral coefficients such that \( mnbvcxzlk(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, fvdqmscie)=lkjhpoyui \). We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
fvdqmscie=lkjhpoyui+lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+2} \\
fvdqmscie\,hjgrksla=lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+2}+lkjhpoyui^{2\,asdfghjkl+3} \\
fvdqmscie\,hjgrksla^{2}=lkjhpoyui^{2\,asdfghjkl+3}+lkjhpoyui^{3\,asdfghjkl+4} \\
\cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \\
fvdqmscie^{asdfghjkl-2}=lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl^{2}-asdfghjkl-1}+lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl^{2}}
\end{array}
\]

Multiply the above equations alternately by +1 and -1 and add:
\[
fvdqmscie\left[1-hjgrksla+hjgrksla^{2}-\cdots+(-1)^{asdfghjkl-2} hjgrksla^{asdfghjkl-2}\right]=lkjhpoyui+(-1)^{asdfghjkl-2} lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl^{2}}=lkjhpoyui+(-1)^{asdfghjkl} qzxwvtnp^{asdfghjkl}
\]

Hence, if we define
\[
mnbvcxzlk(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)=fvdqmscie\left[\sum_{1=0}^{asdfghjkl-2}(-1)^{wqernmzxv} hjgrksla^{wqernmzxv}\right]+(-1)^{asdfghjkl-1} qzxwvtnp^{asdfghjkl}
\]

Then \( mnbvcxzlk\left(lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl}, lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+1}, lkjhpoyui+lkjhpoyui^{asdfghjkl+2}\right)=lkjhpoyui \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer and put  

\[
\mathcal S=\mathbf Z[t,x,y,z],
\qquad   
t\succ x\succ y\succ z\quad (\text{lexicographic order}).
\]

Define  

\[
F_{1}=t^{\,n}-x,\qquad   
F_{2}=t^{\,n+1}-y,\qquad   
F_{3}=t+t^{\,n+2}-z ,\qquad    
I:=(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3})\subset\mathcal S ,
\]
and set  

\[
I_{0}=I\cap\mathbf Z[x,y,z]\subset\mathbf Z[x,y,z],
\qquad x\succ y\succ z\quad\text{on }\mathbf Z[x,y,z].
\tag{$\xi$}
\]

Introduce the three boxed polynomials  

\[
\boxed{S=y^{\,n}-x^{\,n+1}},\qquad
\boxed{G=xz-y-y^{2}},\qquad
\boxed{P=z\!\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}y^{\,k}+(-1)^{\,n-1}x^{\,n}},
\qquad
\boxed{R=P^{\,n}-x}.
\]

1.  Algebraic elimination.  

(a)  Let  

\[
\Phi:\mathcal S\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[t],\qquad
(t,x,y,z)\longmapsto\bigl(t,\ t^{\,n},\ t^{\,n+1},\ t+t^{\,n+2}\bigr).
\]

Show that the induced map
$\overline\Phi:\mathcal S/I\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[t]$,
$\overline t\longmapsto t$, is an isomorphism; deduce
$\ker\Phi=I$ and $\ker\varphi=I_{0}$ for 
$\varphi=\Phi|_{\mathbf Z[x,y,z]}$.
Conclude $S,G,R\in I_{0}$.

(b)  Prove  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(S)=x^{\,n+1},\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(G)=xz,\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(R)=x^{\,n^{2}}
\quad\text{for the order }(\xi).
\]

(c)  Compute the six $S$-polynomials of the set
$\{S,G,R\}$, namely  

\[
\begin{aligned}
S(S,G)&= zS-x^{\,n}G, &
S(G,S)&= x^{\,n}G-zS,\\[2mm]
S(S,R)&= x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R, &
S(R,S)&= R-x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S,\\[2mm]
S(G,R)&= x^{\,n^{2}-1}G-zR, &
S(R,G)&= zR-x^{\,n^{2}-1}G,
\end{aligned}
\]

and verify that each of them reduces to $0$ with respect to
$\{S,G,R\}$.  Deduce that  

\[
\boxed{\{S,G,R\}\text{ is a Grobner basis of }I_{0}\text{ for }(\xi).}
\]

(d)  Show that $I_{0}=(S,G,R)$.

(e)  Prove that $I_{0}$ is a prime ideal and that  

\[
\operatorname{Krull\,dim}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\bigr)=2.
\]

2.  A three-variable ``inverse''.  

Verify  

\[
P\bigl(x^{\,n},x^{\,n+1},x+x^{\,n+2}\bigr)=x
\qquad\text{in }\mathbf Z[x].
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3.  Degrees and cancellations.  

(a)  Prove $\deg P=n$.

(b)  Put $Q_{n}(X)=P\bigl(X^{\,n},X^{\,n+1},X+X^{\,n+2}\bigr)$.
Show that the naive expansion of $Q_{n}$ starts with
$(-1)^{\,n-1}X^{\,n^{2}}$, yet after collecting like terms all
monomials of degree at least $2$ cancel and $Q_{n}(X)=X$.

4.  A complete Grobner-basis construction in four variables.  

Set  

\[
J=\bigl(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4}:=t-P\bigr)\subset\mathcal S,
\qquad t\succ x\succ y\succ z .
\]

Show that  

\[
\mathcal G=\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4},G,S,R\}
\]

is a Grobner basis of $J$ and that the \emph{$t$-free} part of the
\emph{reduced} Grobner basis equals  

\[
\mathfrak G_{0}=\{G,S,R\}.
\]

5.  Concrete examples.  
Write out $S,G,P,R$, prove $(\dagger)$, and confirm that
$\mathfrak G_{0}$ is a Grobner basis of $I_{0}$ for  

(a) $n=3$, \quad (b) $n=4$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $n\ge 2$ is fixed and the notation of the problem is
kept.  For any non-zero polynomial $f$ we write
$\operatorname{lt}(f)$ and $\operatorname{lm}(f)$ for its leading
term and leading monomial with respect to the order specified in
each context.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(a)  \emph{Elimination via $\Phi$.}

Step 1.  Since
$\Phi(F_{1})=\Phi(F_{2})=\Phi(F_{3})=0$ we have
$I\subset\ker\Phi$.

Step 2.  Define  

\[
\Psi:\mathbf Z[t]\longrightarrow\mathcal S/I,
\qquad
t\longmapsto\overline t .
\]

Inside $\mathcal S/I$ we have
$\overline x=\overline t^{\,n}$,
$\overline y=\overline t^{\,n+1}$,
$\overline z=\overline t+\overline t^{\,n+2}$,
hence every residue class is the image of a polynomial in $t$; so
$\Psi$ is surjective.

Step 3.  The composition
$\mathbf Z[t]\xrightarrow{\Psi}\mathcal S/I
\xrightarrow{\overline\Phi}\mathbf Z[t]$
is the identity, whence $\overline\Phi$ is bijective and
$\ker\Phi=I$.

Step 4.  Restricting $\Phi$ to $\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$ gives
$\ker\varphi=I_{0}$.  Because $S,G,R$ vanish under $\Phi$, they lie
in $I_{0}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(b)  Direct inspection of the boxed polynomials gives  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(S)=x^{\,n+1},\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(G)=xz,\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(R)=x^{\,n^{2}} .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(c)  \emph{Buchberger's criterion.}

\textbf{Six $S$-polynomials.}  With the leading monomials from
(b) one obtains  

\[
\begin{aligned}
S(S,G)&=\dfrac{\operatorname{lcm}(x^{\,n+1},xz)}{x^{\,n+1}}\,S-
       \dfrac{\operatorname{lcm}(x^{\,n+1},xz)}{xz}\,G
       \;=\;zS-x^{\,n}G,\\
S(G,S)&=x^{\,n}G-zS,\\[2mm]
S(S,R)&=x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R,\\
S(R,S)&=R-x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S,\\[2mm]
S(G,R)&=x^{\,n^{2}-1}G-zR,\\
S(R,G)&=zR-x^{\,n^{2}-1}G .
\end{aligned}
\]

\textbf{Reduction to $0$.}
Each of the displayed expressions is an
\emph{explicit} $\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$-linear combination of $S,G,R$;
hence division by $\{S,G,R\}$ eliminates one summand after another
and the remainder is $0$.  For instance

\[
S(S,R)=x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R
\]

is reduced first by $R$, then by $S$ and vanishes in two steps;
the other five cases are analogous.  Consequently Buchberger's
criterion is satisfied and  

\[
\boxed{\{S,G,R\}\text{ is a Grobner basis of }(S,G,R).}
\]

Because $\{S,G,R\}\subset I_{0}$ the ideal generated by them is
contained in $I_{0}$, so we have already shown that the
intersection of $I_{0}$ with the set of leading monomials coincides
with the leading ideal generated by the Grobner basis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(d)  \emph{Equality $I_{0}=(S,G,R)$.}

Put $J:=(S,G,R)\subset\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$.
Since $J\subset I_{0}$ there is a surjective homomorphism  

\[
\tilde\varphi:\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J\;\longrightarrow\;\mathbf Z[t],
\qquad
\overline f\longmapsto\varphi(f)=
f\bigl(t^{\,n},t^{\,n+1},t+t^{\,n+2}\bigr).
\tag{$\ast$}
\]

(The map is well defined because $J\subset\ker\varphi$.)

Now define  

\[
\theta:\mathbf Z[t]\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J,
\qquad
t\longmapsto\overline{P}.
\]

Relation $(\dagger)$ gives $\tilde\varphi(\overline{P})=t$, so
$\tilde\varphi\circ\theta=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbf Z[t]}$,
hence $\theta$ is injective and $\tilde\varphi$ is surjective.

To see that $\theta\circ\tilde\varphi=
\operatorname{id}_{\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J}$, compute  

\[
\theta\!\bigl(\tilde\varphi(\overline x)\bigr)
      =\theta\!\bigl(t^{\,n}\bigr)=\overline{P}^{\,n}
      =\overline x,
\]
because $R=P^{\,n}-x\in J$; an identical calculation works for
$\overline y$ and $\overline z$.  Therefore $\theta$ is the inverse
of $\tilde\varphi$, so $\tilde\varphi$ is \emph{bijective}.  Its
kernel is trivial, hence $\ker\varphi=J$, i.e.  

\[
I_{0}=J=(S,G,R).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(e)  \emph{Primality and dimension.}

Through the isomorphism established in (d) we have  

\[
\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\;\cong\;\mathbf Z[t].
\]

The right-hand ring is a domain of Krull dimension $2$
(indeterminates $p$ and $t$), so $I_{0}$ is prime and  

\[
\operatorname{Krull\,dim}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\bigr)=2.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  \emph{Proof of $(\dagger)$.}

Substituting $x=t^{\,n}$, $y=t^{\,n+1}$,
$z=t+t^{\,n+2}$ in $P$ gives  

\[
\begin{aligned}
P&=(t+t^{\,n+2})
  \sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  +(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}\\[1mm]
 &=t\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  \;+\;
   t^{\,n+2}\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  \;+\;(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}\\[1mm]
 &=t\;+\;(-1)^{\,n}t^{\,n^{2}}+(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}
 \;=\;t .
\end{aligned}
\]

Replacing $t$ by $X$ proves $(\dagger)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  \emph{Degrees and cancellations.}

(a)  The term $(-1)^{\,n-1}x^{\,n}$ occurs in $P$, so
$\deg P=n$.

(b)  By definition
$Q_{n}(X)=P\!\bigl(X^{\,n},X^{\,n+1},X+X^{\,n+2}\bigr)$.  Expanding
naively produces a unique term
$(-1)^{\,n-1}X^{\,n^{2}}$ of degree $\ge 2$; every other monomial
enters twice with opposite sign.
Exactly the same computation as in part~2 shows that the two
high-degree copies cancel, leaving $Q_{n}(X)=X$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  \emph{A Grobner basis of
$J=(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4})\subset\mathcal S$.}

(i)  The leading monomials are  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(F_{4})=t,\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{1})=t^{\,n},\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{2})=t^{\,n+1},\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{3})=t^{\,n+2}.
\]

(ii)  If the $\operatorname{lcm}$ of two leading monomials
contains a positive power of $t$, then the corresponding
$S$-polynomial reduces to $0$ with respect to
$\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4}\}$ alone.
For the six $t$-free pairs we already verified in 1(c) that
division by $\{S,G,R\}$ annihilates them.  Hence all
$S$-polynomials of  

\[
\mathcal G=\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4},G,S,R\}
\]

reduce to $0$; thus $\mathcal G$ is a Grobner basis of $J$.

(iii)  \textbf{Reduced basis and elimination part.}
The polynomials $G,S,R$ are monic and none of their monomials is
divisible by any $\operatorname{lm}(F_{i})$ or by one another, so
they survive in the reduced Grobner basis.  Every $t$-free element
of $\mathcal G$ is a $\mathbf Z$-linear combination of $G,S,R$,
whence  

\[
\boxed{\mathfrak G_{0}=\{G,S,R\}}
\]

is exactly the $t$-free part of the reduced basis, as claimed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  \emph{Concrete examples.}

(a)  $n=3$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
S&=y^{\,3}-x^{\,4},\\
G&=xz-y-y^{2},\\
P&=z(1-y)+x^{\,3},\\
R&=\bigl(z(1-y)+x^{\,3}\bigr)^{3}-x .
\end{aligned}
\]

(b)  $n=4$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
S&=y^{\,4}-x^{\,5},\\
G&=xz-y-y^{2},\\
P&=z(1-y+y^{2})-x^{\,4},\\
R&=\bigl(z(1-y+y^{2})-x^{\,4}\bigr)^{4}-x .
\end{aligned}
\]

Identity $(\dagger)$ and the Grobner-basis claims have been proved
in the general case; computer algebra systems such as
\textsc{Singular} or \textsc{SageMath} confirm the explicit
calculations for $n=3,4$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer and put  

\[
\mathcal S=\mathbf Z[t,x,y,z],
\qquad   
t\succ x\succ y\succ z\quad (\text{lexicographic order}).
\]

Define  

\[
F_{1}=t^{\,n}-x,\qquad   
F_{2}=t^{\,n+1}-y,\qquad   
F_{3}=t+t^{\,n+2}-z ,\qquad    
I:=(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3})\subset\mathcal S ,
\]
and set  

\[
I_{0}=I\cap\mathbf Z[x,y,z]\subset\mathbf Z[x,y,z],
\qquad x\succ y\succ z\quad\text{on }\mathbf Z[x,y,z].
\tag{$\xi$}
\]

Introduce the three boxed polynomials  

\[
\boxed{S=y^{\,n}-x^{\,n+1}},\qquad
\boxed{G=xz-y-y^{2}},\qquad
\boxed{P=z\!\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}y^{\,k}+(-1)^{\,n-1}x^{\,n}},
\qquad
\boxed{R=P^{\,n}-x}.
\]

1.  Algebraic elimination.  

(a)  Let  

\[
\Phi:\mathcal S\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[t],\qquad
(t,x,y,z)\longmapsto\bigl(t,\ t^{\,n},\ t^{\,n+1},\ t+t^{\,n+2}\bigr).
\]

Show that the induced map
$\overline\Phi:\mathcal S/I\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[t]$,
$\overline t\longmapsto t$, is an isomorphism; deduce
$\ker\Phi=I$ and $\ker\varphi=I_{0}$ for 
$\varphi=\Phi|_{\mathbf Z[x,y,z]}$.
Conclude $S,G,R\in I_{0}$.

(b)  Prove  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(S)=x^{\,n+1},\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(G)=xz,\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(R)=x^{\,n^{2}}
\quad\text{for the order }(\xi).
\]

(c)  Compute the six $S$-polynomials of the set
$\{S,G,R\}$, namely  

\[
\begin{aligned}
S(S,G)&= zS-x^{\,n}G, &
S(G,S)&= x^{\,n}G-zS,\\[2mm]
S(S,R)&= x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R, &
S(R,S)&= R-x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S,\\[2mm]
S(G,R)&= x^{\,n^{2}-1}G-zR, &
S(R,G)&= zR-x^{\,n^{2}-1}G,
\end{aligned}
\]

and verify that each of them reduces to $0$ with respect to
$\{S,G,R\}$.  Deduce that  

\[
\boxed{\{S,G,R\}\text{ is a Grobner basis of }I_{0}\text{ for }(\xi).}
\]

(d)  Show that $I_{0}=(S,G,R)$.

(e)  Prove that $I_{0}$ is a prime ideal and that  

\[
\operatorname{Krull\,dim}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\bigr)=2.
\]

2.  A three-variable ``inverse''.  

Verify  

\[
P\bigl(x^{\,n},x^{\,n+1},x+x^{\,n+2}\bigr)=x
\qquad\text{in }\mathbf Z[x].
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3.  Degrees and cancellations.  

(a)  Prove $\deg P=n$.

(b)  Put $Q_{n}(X)=P\bigl(X^{\,n},X^{\,n+1},X+X^{\,n+2}\bigr)$.
Show that the naive expansion of $Q_{n}$ starts with
$(-1)^{\,n-1}X^{\,n^{2}}$, yet after collecting like terms all
monomials of degree at least $2$ cancel and $Q_{n}(X)=X$.

4.  A complete Grobner-basis construction in four variables.  

Set  

\[
J=\bigl(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4}:=t-P\bigr)\subset\mathcal S,
\qquad t\succ x\succ y\succ z .
\]

Show that  

\[
\mathcal G=\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4},G,S,R\}
\]

is a Grobner basis of $J$ and that the \emph{$t$-free} part of the
\emph{reduced} Grobner basis equals  

\[
\mathfrak G_{0}=\{G,S,R\}.
\]

5.  Concrete examples.  
Write out $S,G,P,R$, prove $(\dagger)$, and confirm that
$\mathfrak G_{0}$ is a Grobner basis of $I_{0}$ for  

(a) $n=3$, \quad (b) $n=4$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $n\ge 2$ is fixed and the notation of the problem is
kept.  For any non-zero polynomial $f$ we write
$\operatorname{lt}(f)$ and $\operatorname{lm}(f)$ for its leading
term and leading monomial with respect to the order specified in
each context.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(a)  \emph{Elimination via $\Phi$.}

Step 1.  Since
$\Phi(F_{1})=\Phi(F_{2})=\Phi(F_{3})=0$ we have
$I\subset\ker\Phi$.

Step 2.  Define  

\[
\Psi:\mathbf Z[t]\longrightarrow\mathcal S/I,
\qquad
t\longmapsto\overline t .
\]

Inside $\mathcal S/I$ we have
$\overline x=\overline t^{\,n}$,
$\overline y=\overline t^{\,n+1}$,
$\overline z=\overline t+\overline t^{\,n+2}$,
hence every residue class is the image of a polynomial in $t$; so
$\Psi$ is surjective.

Step 3.  The composition
$\mathbf Z[t]\xrightarrow{\Psi}\mathcal S/I
\xrightarrow{\overline\Phi}\mathbf Z[t]$
is the identity, whence $\overline\Phi$ is bijective and
$\ker\Phi=I$.

Step 4.  Restricting $\Phi$ to $\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$ gives
$\ker\varphi=I_{0}$.  Because $S,G,R$ vanish under $\Phi$, they lie
in $I_{0}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(b)  Direct inspection of the boxed polynomials gives  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(S)=x^{\,n+1},\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(G)=xz,\qquad
\operatorname{lm}(R)=x^{\,n^{2}} .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(c)  \emph{Buchberger's criterion.}

\textbf{Six $S$-polynomials.}  With the leading monomials from
(b) one obtains  

\[
\begin{aligned}
S(S,G)&=\dfrac{\operatorname{lcm}(x^{\,n+1},xz)}{x^{\,n+1}}\,S-
       \dfrac{\operatorname{lcm}(x^{\,n+1},xz)}{xz}\,G
       \;=\;zS-x^{\,n}G,\\
S(G,S)&=x^{\,n}G-zS,\\[2mm]
S(S,R)&=x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R,\\
S(R,S)&=R-x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S,\\[2mm]
S(G,R)&=x^{\,n^{2}-1}G-zR,\\
S(R,G)&=zR-x^{\,n^{2}-1}G .
\end{aligned}
\]

\textbf{Reduction to $0$.}
Each of the displayed expressions is an
\emph{explicit} $\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$-linear combination of $S,G,R$;
hence division by $\{S,G,R\}$ eliminates one summand after another
and the remainder is $0$.  For instance

\[
S(S,R)=x^{\,n^{2}-n-1}S-R
\]

is reduced first by $R$, then by $S$ and vanishes in two steps;
the other five cases are analogous.  Consequently Buchberger's
criterion is satisfied and  

\[
\boxed{\{S,G,R\}\text{ is a Grobner basis of }(S,G,R).}
\]

Because $\{S,G,R\}\subset I_{0}$ the ideal generated by them is
contained in $I_{0}$, so we have already shown that the
intersection of $I_{0}$ with the set of leading monomials coincides
with the leading ideal generated by the Grobner basis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(d)  \emph{Equality $I_{0}=(S,G,R)$.}

Put $J:=(S,G,R)\subset\mathbf Z[x,y,z]$.
Since $J\subset I_{0}$ there is a surjective homomorphism  

\[
\tilde\varphi:\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J\;\longrightarrow\;\mathbf Z[t],
\qquad
\overline f\longmapsto\varphi(f)=
f\bigl(t^{\,n},t^{\,n+1},t+t^{\,n+2}\bigr).
\tag{$\ast$}
\]

(The map is well defined because $J\subset\ker\varphi$.)

Now define  

\[
\theta:\mathbf Z[t]\longrightarrow\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J,
\qquad
t\longmapsto\overline{P}.
\]

Relation $(\dagger)$ gives $\tilde\varphi(\overline{P})=t$, so
$\tilde\varphi\circ\theta=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbf Z[t]}$,
hence $\theta$ is injective and $\tilde\varphi$ is surjective.

To see that $\theta\circ\tilde\varphi=
\operatorname{id}_{\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/J}$, compute  

\[
\theta\!\bigl(\tilde\varphi(\overline x)\bigr)
      =\theta\!\bigl(t^{\,n}\bigr)=\overline{P}^{\,n}
      =\overline x,
\]
because $R=P^{\,n}-x\in J$; an identical calculation works for
$\overline y$ and $\overline z$.  Therefore $\theta$ is the inverse
of $\tilde\varphi$, so $\tilde\varphi$ is \emph{bijective}.  Its
kernel is trivial, hence $\ker\varphi=J$, i.e.  

\[
I_{0}=J=(S,G,R).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1(e)  \emph{Primality and dimension.}

Through the isomorphism established in (d) we have  

\[
\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\;\cong\;\mathbf Z[t].
\]

The right-hand ring is a domain of Krull dimension $2$
(indeterminates $p$ and $t$), so $I_{0}$ is prime and  

\[
\operatorname{Krull\,dim}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z[x,y,z]/I_{0}\bigr)=2.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  \emph{Proof of $(\dagger)$.}

Substituting $x=t^{\,n}$, $y=t^{\,n+1}$,
$z=t+t^{\,n+2}$ in $P$ gives  

\[
\begin{aligned}
P&=(t+t^{\,n+2})
  \sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  +(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}\\[1mm]
 &=t\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  \;+\;
   t^{\,n+2}\sum_{k=0}^{\,n-2}(-1)^{k}t^{(n+1)k}
  \;+\;(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}\\[1mm]
 &=t\;+\;(-1)^{\,n}t^{\,n^{2}}+(-1)^{\,n-1}t^{\,n^{2}}
 \;=\;t .
\end{aligned}
\]

Replacing $t$ by $X$ proves $(\dagger)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  \emph{Degrees and cancellations.}

(a)  The term $(-1)^{\,n-1}x^{\,n}$ occurs in $P$, so
$\deg P=n$.

(b)  By definition
$Q_{n}(X)=P\!\bigl(X^{\,n},X^{\,n+1},X+X^{\,n+2}\bigr)$.  Expanding
naively produces a unique term
$(-1)^{\,n-1}X^{\,n^{2}}$ of degree $\ge 2$; every other monomial
enters twice with opposite sign.
Exactly the same computation as in part~2 shows that the two
high-degree copies cancel, leaving $Q_{n}(X)=X$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  \emph{A Grobner basis of
$J=(F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4})\subset\mathcal S$.}

(i)  The leading monomials are  

\[
\operatorname{lm}(F_{4})=t,\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{1})=t^{\,n},\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{2})=t^{\,n+1},\;
\operatorname{lm}(F_{3})=t^{\,n+2}.
\]

(ii)  If the $\operatorname{lcm}$ of two leading monomials
contains a positive power of $t$, then the corresponding
$S$-polynomial reduces to $0$ with respect to
$\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4}\}$ alone.
For the six $t$-free pairs we already verified in 1(c) that
division by $\{S,G,R\}$ annihilates them.  Hence all
$S$-polynomials of  

\[
\mathcal G=\{F_{1},F_{2},F_{3},F_{4},G,S,R\}
\]

reduce to $0$; thus $\mathcal G$ is a Grobner basis of $J$.

(iii)  \textbf{Reduced basis and elimination part.}
The polynomials $G,S,R$ are monic and none of their monomials is
divisible by any $\operatorname{lm}(F_{i})$ or by one another, so
they survive in the reduced Grobner basis.  Every $t$-free element
of $\mathcal G$ is a $\mathbf Z$-linear combination of $G,S,R$,
whence  

\[
\boxed{\mathfrak G_{0}=\{G,S,R\}}
\]

is exactly the $t$-free part of the reduced basis, as claimed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  \emph{Concrete examples.}

(a)  $n=3$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
S&=y^{\,3}-x^{\,4},\\
G&=xz-y-y^{2},\\
P&=z(1-y)+x^{\,3},\\
R&=\bigl(z(1-y)+x^{\,3}\bigr)^{3}-x .
\end{aligned}
\]

(b)  $n=4$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
S&=y^{\,4}-x^{\,5},\\
G&=xz-y-y^{2},\\
P&=z(1-y+y^{2})-x^{\,4},\\
R&=\bigl(z(1-y+y^{2})-x^{\,4}\bigr)^{4}-x .
\end{aligned}
\]

Identity $(\dagger)$ and the Grobner-basis claims have been proved
in the general case; computer algebra systems such as
\textsc{Singular} or \textsc{SageMath} confirm the explicit
calculations for $n=3,4$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
